quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Transcription"?

options.text: Germline mutations (in eggs/sperm) are passed to offspring; somatic mutations affect only the individual and are not inherited
Speciation occurring within the same geographic area, often through polyploidy (plants) or habitat/behavioral specialization
The main replication enzyme in E. coli; synthesizes DNA 5'→3' by adding nucleotides complementary to the template strand
RNA polymerase reads the DNA template strand 3'→5' and synthesizes complementary mRNA 5'→3'; occurs in the nucleus in eukaryotes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Transcription" is defined as: RNA polymerase reads the DNA template strand 3'→5' and synthesizes complementary mRNA 5'→3'; occurs in the nucleus in eukaryotes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Promoter"?

options.text: Genes on the X chromosome; males (XY) express all X-linked alleles; conditions like hemophilia and color blindness are more common in males
The number of different species in an area; generally increases with area, decreases with latitude, and increases toward the tropics
A grid used to predict genotype and phenotype ratios in offspring from a particular cross
A DNA sequence upstream of a gene (e.g., TATA box) where RNA polymerase and transcription factors bind to initiate transcription

quiz_questions.explanation: "Promoter" is defined as: A DNA sequence upstream of a gene (e.g., TATA box) where RNA polymerase and transcription factors bind to initiate transcription. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Pre-mRNA processing"?

options.text: 2pq; the proportion of heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele in a population, estimable from disease frequency (q²)
Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., Iᴬ Iᴮ blood type produces both A and B antigens)
v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]); describes the relationship between substrate concentration and reaction rate for many enzymes
5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Pre-mRNA processing" is defined as: 5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Introns and exons"?

options.text: The number of individuals contributing genetically to the next generation; usually much smaller than census size due to unequal sex ratios, variance in reproductive success
The monomer of proteins: a central carbon bonded to an amino group (-NH₂), carboxyl group (-COOH), hydrogen, and variable R-group
ATP production driven by the proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane; H⁺ flows through ATP synthase from lumen to stroma
Introns are non-coding sequences removed by splicing; exons are coding sequences that are joined together in mature mRNA

quiz_questions.explanation: "Introns and exons" is defined as: Introns are non-coding sequences removed by splicing; exons are coding sequences that are joined together in mature mRNA. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Alternative splicing"?

options.text: Different combinations of exons are joined to produce multiple mRNA variants (and proteins) from a single gene
Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids
The main replication enzyme in E. coli; synthesizes DNA 5'→3' by adding nucleotides complementary to the template strand
Control points at G1, G2, and M where the cell evaluates conditions before proceeding; can halt the cycle if conditions are unfavorable

quiz_questions.explanation: "Alternative splicing" is defined as: Different combinations of exons are joined to produce multiple mRNA variants (and proteins) from a single gene. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "RNA polymerase reads the DNA template strand 3'→5' and synthesizes complementary mRNA 5'→3'; occurs in the nucleus in eukaryotes"?

options.text: Biodiversity
microRNA (miRNA)
Transcription
Reproductive isolation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Transcription".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A DNA sequence upstream of a gene (e.g., TATA box) where RNA polymerase and transcription factors bind to initiate transcription"?

options.text: Glycolysis
Promoter
Net primary productivity (NPP)
Habitat destruction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Promoter".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes"?

options.text: Pre-mRNA processing
Hydrogen bond
Crossing over
Allele frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Pre-mRNA processing".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Introns are non-coding sequences removed by splicing; exons are coding sequences that are joined together in mature mRNA"?

options.text: Introns and exons
Angiogenesis
Aerobic vs. anaerobic
Central dogma

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Introns and exons".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Different combinations of exons are joined to produce multiple mRNA variants (and proteins) from a single gene"?

options.text: Alternative splicing
Vestigial structures
Acetyl-CoA
CRISPR-Cas9

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Alternative splicing".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about DNA Replication and Transcription is accurate?

options.text: DNA Replication and Transcription describes how genetic information stored in DNA is copied (replication) and read into mRNA (transcription).

Interspecific competition (competition exclusion principle or resource partitioning), predation (including coevolutionary arms races), mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism shape community structure.
Translation is the process by which ribosomes synthesize proteins from mRNA templates.
Adaptive radiation (rapid diversification from a single ancestor into multiple ecological niches) often follows mass extinctions or colonization of new environments.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Replication and Transcription as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of DNA Replication and Transcription?

options.text: The enzyme RuBisCO catalyzes carbon fixation (CO₂ + RuBP → 2 molecules of 3-PGA), making it the most abundant protein on Earth.
Transcription is catalyzed by RNA polymerase, which reads the template strand 3'→5' and synthesizes mRNA 5'→3'.
Evolution and Evidence establishes that populations change over time through multiple converging lines of evidence.
Translation is the process by which ribosomes synthesize proteins from mRNA templates.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Replication and Transcription as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about DNA Replication and Transcription?

options.text: Monohybrid crosses (Aa × Aa → 3:1 phenotypic ratio) and dihybrid crosses (AaBb × AaBb → 9:3:3:1 ratio) predict offspring ratios, testable with chi-square analysis.
In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes processing: 5' cap addition, 3' poly-A tail, and splicing to remove introns and join exons.
Cellular Respiration: Krebs Cycle (citric acid cycle) occurs in the mitochondrial matrix when pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA (producing CO₂ and NADH), which then enters the cycle.

The three stages—reception (ligand binding), transduction (signal relay/amplification through phosphorylation cascades), and response (gene expression, enzyme activation, or cell movement)—allow cells to respond to their environment.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Replication and Transcription as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of DNA Replication and Transcription?

options.text: Enzyme specificity arises from the precise 3D shape of the active site, which is determined by the amino acid sequence.
The overall equation is C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ~30-32 ATP.
Alternative splicing enables one gene to produce multiple protein variants.
Current extinction rates are 100-1,000 times higher than background rates due to habitat destruction, invasive species, overexploitation, pollution, and climate change (HIPPO threats).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Replication and Transcription as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on DNA Replication and Transcription?

options.text: Logistic growth (dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K) incorporates carrying capacity K and produces S-shaped curves.
The Knudson two-hit hypothesis explains that both copies of a tumor suppressor must be inactivated for cancer to develop.
Cell Division (Mitosis and Meiosis) are the two types of nuclear division.
DNA Replication and Transcription describes how genetic information stored in DNA is copied (replication) and read into mRNA (transcription).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Replication and Transcription as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of DNA Replication and Transcription?

options.text: Hemophilia in European royal families: X-linked recessive inheritance traced through pedigrees from Queen Victoria to multiple royal houses
Alternative splicing producing over 100,000 different proteins from only ~20,000 human genes, explaining how gene count doesn't correlate with organism complexity
Endosymbiotic theory evidence: mitochondria and chloroplasts have double membranes, own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, and replicate independently
Amazon deforestation averaging ~10,000 km² per year, threatening the most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystem with an estimated 10% of all species on Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Replication and Transcription.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of DNA Replication and Transcription?

options.text: α-amanitin (death cap mushroom toxin) inhibiting RNA polymerase II, blocking transcription and causing fatal liver failure within days
Darwin's finches on the Galápagos Islands illustrating adaptive radiation: 14 species with different beak shapes evolved from a single ancestor to exploit different food sources
Prion diseases (mad cow, CJD) where misfolded proteins induce normal proteins to refold incorrectly, demonstrating the critical importance of protein conformation
Capillary action in plant xylem vessels transporting water against gravity from roots to leaves through cohesion-adhesion-tension

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Replication and Transcription.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is DNA Replication and Transcription applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: RT-PCR diagnostic tests detecting viral RNA (COVID-19) by reverse-transcribing RNA to DNA and amplifying specific sequences
Antibiotic targeting of bacterial ribosomes: tetracycline blocks the A-site, chloramphenicol inhibits peptidyl transferase—both exploit differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic ribosomes
Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
Madagascar's unique biodiversity (90% of species found nowhere else) explained by 88 million years of isolation after separating from Africa and India

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Replication and Transcription.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of DNA Replication and Transcription?

options.text: Alternative splicing producing over 100,000 different proteins from only ~20,000 human genes, explaining how gene count doesn't correlate with organism complexity
Artificial selection in agriculture: selective breeding transforming teosinte (small, hard kernels) into modern corn over thousands of years of human-directed selection
Down syndrome (trisomy 21) caused by nondisjunction during meiosis, resulting in an extra copy of chromosome 21 in all cells
Darwin's finch beak evolution on Daphne Major Island: drought years selected for larger beaks (to crack harder seeds), directly observed by Peter and Rosemary Grant over 40 years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Replication and Transcription.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows DNA Replication and Transcription in practice?

options.text: Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
α-amanitin (death cap mushroom toxin) inhibiting RNA polymerase II, blocking transcription and causing fatal liver failure within days
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence
DNA profiling (forensic fingerprinting) using short tandem repeat (STR) polymorphisms for criminal identification with error rates below 1 in a billion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Replication and Transcription.
